speaker: interviewee
text: Gunther, can you tell me more about camp? Describe it a little bit, the conditions of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember it very well. The first thing that impressed me as a little 10-year-old was-- 11-year-old-- was that the trains were very far away from the barracks and they were elevated above the ground and there were pots standing underneath that you had to target. In the beginning, there were no seats, they were French toilets, so you had to balance yourself. And they were always very dirty.

speaker: interviewee
text: The odor was unbelievable because everything was open. The reason why they were elevated is that they had a system of trains that came around the camp run by inmates who would empty these buckets every day and bring new ones. The other thing that I remember well is there was no hot water, everything was cold water. You had to wash with cold water. There were hours when you could or could not go to wash. There were certain areas where there were open air basins. This was in the wintertime. The Pyrenees can get very cold. And that's where we had to wash ourselves. Laundry had to be washed there, too. They gave you certain hours when you could do that. In the camp, each-- each [INAUDIBLE] had its own basic administration, which was more or less inmate run. A woman was chief of the [INAUDIBLE], they had their own kitchen and women were doing the cooking. We had a doctor in each [INAUDIBLE] in a hospital barracks and he would treat whoever needed help. The biggest problem was dysentery, and it was deadly. One of the things that comes to mind most vividly was when we got there, there were mounds in the middle of the [INAUDIBLE], about, oh, three to four feet tall, made of dirt. And no one ever could figure out why these mounds were there. But then when it started to rain, we suddenly realized what they were because as the dirt washed away, arms and legs came out. These were Spanish prisoners who had died and they had made underground graves for them, mass graves, and just covered them with dirt. And so the health situation was tremendously bad. The food-- my Aunt Ruth was in the barracks and was barracks chief. She always managed to get involved in these kind of things. She would have to report every day for every meal how many people were in the barracks and then she would get food accordingly. In the morning, it was a watery coffee, that's it. At noon, you got a soup which was made of rutabaga that you couldn't find and there were some chickpeas floating on top. The chickpeas were divided according to a list, one per person as far as the list went, and then the next day if there were four chickpeas on years on the soup that day, then the next four people on the list got a chickpea each. And then we got a ration of bread, which was about 250 grams. And that was the bread for the day. Then in the evening, we got another pot of soup. And once in a while when the weather was really bad and the mud was very thick, sometimes the women carrying the soup between and two of them with probably two handles would stumble and some of the soup would spill. And if they lost it, that's just too bad. There was just no soup that day. After a while, the decision was made that the children needed more food. They also made the decision that the children needed to be away from their parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So two barracks were set up, one for boys, one for girls, in which we eventually got beds and we slept there. But we went to see our mothers every day. There we got the same rations plus an extra 10% bread plus each week a cup measure of lard and a cup measure of brown sugar. This was supplemental food. Other than that, we got the same rations everybody else did. We ate it there and we kept our food there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We slept there. And then also they brought in tables and we started having school. Like with everything that Jewish people do, educating the children is always the priority. It was here, too. And some of the women who were or were not teachers would start teaching us French. We are now in France, you have to learn French. So gradually, we started learning French, we started-- we learned some other things to keep us busy. The other thing, my father was in [INAUDIBLE] D-- Barrack 13 I think-- and we had free go between the [INAUDIBLE]. So I could visit my father anytime I wanted to. And very frequently, my cousin and I would go visit our fathers. And once in a while, the mothers could go on certain days or with passes to go see the fathers or vice versa. And this was always a big occasion. But I would be with my father at least two or three times a day-- a week-- to visit. The death rate in the camp was awful. People died every night. And what us children started to discover was that in the morning, a truck would pull up and pick up the dead people from the hospital barracks and then that later that morning, that same truck would come and deliver the bread and the meat and the foods and everything else in the camp with the same drivers. Once a very close friend of our family died-- I do not remember who it was-- and my mother insisted that I go with her to the funeral. The cemetery was at the end of the camp and we walked there. It had not been raining that day, so it was relatively dry. And they had a makeshift casket, and being a young, curious child, I looked into the grave. And the grave was filled to within about a foot from the top with water from the ground. And the bodies-- the caskets-- were just laid into the water and then the whole thing was covered with dirt. So it was a very watery grave, and it's something that I don't think I'll ever forget. This total insanity of people living like animals and being treated like animals and not being able to do anything about it, the total lack of power to do anything about not only your own, but your fellow man's condition, was unbelievable. Around Christmas, my aunt who had her mother in Italy at the time found out that their mother had moved to Oleron. She was one of these free spirits. She had been a widow for many, many years and she just traveled wherever she wanted to, or she could. And she had relocated to Oleron and we got passes for my aunt, my cousin, and me, to go to Oleron for a day to visit with her. This was the first day in civilization that I had seen since October 22nd.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever consider trying to escape there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was very heavily guarded. There were double barbed wire all around the camp and guard towers all over. It was not a place where you could easily walk away from. And being in a foreign country, besides, with no one to go to, it would have been extremely difficult to find any way of escaping. The OSE came into play at that point, which was Oeuvre de Secours aux Enfants, a children's organization which was financed from America. And they started coming to see us, help us with extra clothing warmer blankets, things like that. And-- but to escape would at this time had been not thinkable. It was very difficult for my aunt and my cousin and me to come back to camp that night, but we did. Pulling yourself away from such a situation with lots of food and good food and friendly surroundings was very inviting. We came back, and all this time my father and mother were trying to have the papers that were originally executed in Stuttgart moved to Marseille and be reactivated there. In January, we found out that they were there and we started working on trying to get to Marseilles. Meanwhile, the American embassy-- the American consulate-- requested our presence in Marseille to be processed. So coming from both ends, it finally was decided that on February 28-- six-- or 27th-- my father would be transferred to Gurs-- to Le Milles by train, which was a camp outside of Marseille. And my mother and I on March 1st, 1941, were transferred to Marseille by train with a very tearful departure because we knew a lot of people we would never see again, including my cousin and my aunt and uncle. We got to Marseille and we had been told we would be staying at the Hotel Terminus. We get out of the train station and there in front of us is this gorgeous Hotel Terminus. And the police officers who met us there promptly walked us past that beautiful hotel. We realized that was not for us. We walked a long, long ways and wound up at what is called Hotel Teminus du Port, namely near the port of Marseille, in a very scroungy neighborhood. It was a hotel that was occupied by women and children who were trying to emigrate. It was within one city block of the harbor. And we got all of our meals there, such as they were, and we lived there. We had to be in the place between 9:00 PM and 7:00 AM. If we wanted to be out overnight, we had to get a pass. During the day, we could come and go as we wished. And that's how we then went to the consulate and got our papers processed again. Then my father would come in on the trolley from [INAUDIBLE] Provence near camp. He would have to walk about 30 minutes to the nearest tram and then take the tram and come into town. And he would stay at a hotel nearby that we would make reservations for. And this was a time when we would all stay at the hotel and get an overnight pass while we can.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this go on for?

speaker: interviewer
text: At any time during your stay at Gurs or in Marseille, were you able to practice any religious activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gurs, I do not remember any religious things being done. When we got to Marseille, there was another hotel nearby called Hotel [INAUDIBLE], and there they had services every Friday and on the holidays. And we would participate there, as best-- as often as we could. And I don't know about my father at Le Milles. I don't know that. But my mother and I would go whenever we could and participate in the service. It was ultra orthodox, which is not what we were used to. And for me as a child, it was rather boring. But it was services and we went. Then in the beginning of 1942, I started preparing for my bar mitzvah and I was put in touch with a rabbi who headed a more conservative synagogue. It was not a synagogue, it was a-- a prayer room in a storefront kind of thing. And he taught me my [HEBREW], my Torah portion, and helped me to sing, which was a major project. He would play the piano and his son would help on the drums to try and get my ear to hear what they wanted me to sing because I am relatively tone deaf. And I studied there regularly, went there every week when I didn't have school on Wednesday afternoon, and then went there also on Shabbat afternoon to study with this rabbi to get ready for my bar mitzvah. Also, when we got to Marseille, the most urgent priority was learning French because we could not go around Marseille saying we were German because [INAUDIBLE] were hated and we had to say that we were from Alsace-Lorraine, that we were refugees from Alsace-Lorraine. But you can't be from Alsace-Lorraine and not know French. So the committee hired a man to kill his from Alsace-Lorraine spoke no German, and we had sessions five days a week with him learning French, the written French and spoken French. And it was an interesting experience learning from somebody who could not translate for you. And we would act out these different words or bring samples, anything to get him to understand what we were saying. Not only that, but we had dictionaries that we used because we had to have him understand. And as we were learning French, he was learning German. And by September of 1941, we were fluent enough that I was able to go to a public school. And I attended the school near the hotel, which was the boys' school near the cathedral up on the hill in the Harbor District. I had a very understanding teacher who knew what the situation was. And the French theory of learning is memorizing. I was a lousy memorizer, much less in a foreign language, so he was very lenient with that. He helped me with various subject matter and I had a very, very good year in that school. In August of 1942, on the 25th of August, I had my bar mitzvah. My mother had invited her sister from Nimes. This was the family who had lived in Stuttgart. He had ancestors who were French so they immigrated to France and they lived in Nimes, which wasn't very far away. And my other uncle and aunts and Ruth and my cousin, Liesel, were in Camp [INAUDIBLE], which was also an internment camp similar to Gurs. And my uncle was working on a farm and living on the farm while my aunt was in the camp. Liesel had by then been taken to one of the children's camps, so she was away from her. And we wanted them to come, too. And they regretted they could not come. Number one, they didn't have the money for the trip and number two, they wouldn't get that long a furlough. My uncle and aunt from Nimes at one point said, no, they won't come. And then the day before that Friday morning, all of a sudden, he and my cousin show up. My aunt didn't show up. And we found out later it was a letter from my grandparents saying, I hope you go that made them come. There was also a brother of my father's, [? Morris, ?] who came. And then a very good friend of my father's who lived in the area also came. In the last minute, my teacher from Germany, Rabbi Marx, and his wife showed up. And they had made arrangements for a dinner. And they took care of all the arrangements that way in the very last moment. We had Shabbat dinner together in a restaurant and then went to services and then we stayed at our hotel-- my father, my mother, and I-- the same as the other visitors. So we were together. On Saturday morning-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --we went to services-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] --and it probably was the most [SOBBING] important day of my life. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] [CRYING] My father had the same day of his bar mitzvah and I sang the same Torah portion as he had sung just before. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --he stood behind me. [SOBBING] I could feel the pride that he had in what was going on. That afternoon, we had lunch at the hotel with everybody and a discussion started about what was happening. There was rumors around that things were changing. And my father's friend said, I'm going into hiding immediately after I leave here. I want you, meaning my father, to come with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: He also could make arrangements for my mother and me to be immediately smuggled into Switzerland. He said, you have till Monday, you have a pass till Monday night. You'll have a head start, no one will miss you. Have to leave now. I still hear the conversation. My father said, no, I can't do that. Why not? He had no reason, but he could not do it. The conversation of this went on all weekend.

speaker: interviewee
text: And since we had contacts in Switzerland, which is how my grandparents communicated with us from Germany-- these people were friends of Ruth and [INAUDIBLE] mother and father, who was in [INAUDIBLE]. These friends were friends of hers who had become our contact. And everything-- everyone knew if something happens to anybody, this is who you write to. So he said-- so this friend of my father's said, you have these friends. Go there. Get out now. They wouldn't hear of it. The weekend was very pleasant. Everybody left, my father went back to camp. And by the way, I might add that in that time, he had been moved to a labor camp called Miramar, which is also in the same area of the Provence. And he was very happy there because he could do some manual labor other than barbering, which he had been doing in the camps, and which had gotten us some extra income for incidentals that we needed. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was just going to say, just from our pre-interview, you had mentioned that you were in Camp Les Milles.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. OK. My father went back to Miramar, we went back to Hotel Terminus, and on the 3rd of August, we woke up and the whole hotel, the whole block, was surrounded by French militia. And we knew we were in big trouble. It was another time of we should have. We were told to pack, then we were told not to pack. We were given food, but we couldn't eat. My mother had been buying food from the black market periodically, and she had all this food and she was packing it. We didn't know what was happening. And late that night, we were told to pack, that we were leaving the following morning and that we were going to Camp Les Milles. Our luggage was put into one truck, we were put into another truck early in the morning, oh, probably around seven o'clock, and we were driven to Les Milles. Les Milles is a camp that was a brick factory. I've been back there since and it is again a brick factory. It has never changed. It was directly on a railroad line and trains would come in and out of there to deliver prisoners. They didn't even have to go to the railroad station. It was a very primitive place, furiously hot in the summer because there was nowhere to hide. The barracks building itself had some windows in it, not very many. You opened those up and you got a little bit of a breeze, but in most cases, you spent the time in the yard in the hot sun. In the wintertime, it was terribly cold because there was no way of heating it. And as in all the camps, mice and rats and lice were a constant problem. And every time my father came to Marseille, the first thing my mother would do is give him a bath and get him treated for lice. It was just not possible to get away from it. But this is also a health hazard. We get to Les Milles August 4th, 1942, in the heat of the day. And at first they put us separate from the men in a different area. The men from all the camps had since then been brought back to Les Milles also. So we found my father immediately. And then other people who had been rounded up in the various cities also came to Les Milles and the place started filling up with more and more people. We were told that we would be taken elsewhere, they didn't say where. The indication was that it was labor camps. The justification was this, when Germany made peace with France, the French had to supply a certain number of French foreign workers to the industries in Germany. The first ones went. They came back in 1941, the second set went as volunteers. When they-- when the first set then came back, the news of how badly they were treated was told. When in 1942, supposedly they were supposed to send another 100,000, no one would volunteer.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've got to stop that in the next tape.
